title: International Finance and Macroeconomics
gist: This is the NBER International Finance and Macroeconomics program landing page, which LISTS many recent working papers with short abstracts rather than presenting a single paper. Notable findings cluster around dollar/Treasury convenience-yield decoupling, reserve-currency erosion, geoeconomic coercion, tariff effects on inflation expectations and Treasuries, and China imbalances; each insight captures one paper's finding.

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35328) by Jiang, Krishnamurthy, Lustig & Richmond quantifies that losing reserve-currency status costs the US about 1% of GDP per annum in seigniorage, forcing lower household spending and a real depreciation to clear excess US goods.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Puts a GDP price tag on de-dollarization risk — a tail scenario for USD and US asset valuations.
insights.quote: the loss of seigniorage (1% of GDP per annum) makes American households spend less

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35000) by Du, Keerati & Schreger documents a strong decoupling since the early 2010s between the convenience yield on the US dollar and US Treasuries, measured via covered-interest-parity deviations.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Dollar and Treasury 'privilege' are separating — reshapes safe-asset demand and basis-trade/CIP positioning.
insights.quote: We document a strong decoupling between the convenience yield on the U.S. dollar and U.S. Treasuries
insights.locator: Working Paper 35000 — Decoupling Dollar and Treasury Privilege

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34888) by Goldberg & Hannaoui decomposes the dollar share of central-bank FX reserves into two distinct channels, cautioning against reading the aggregate share directly as the dollar's global status.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Refines how to interpret reserve-share headlines that drive de-dollarization narratives.
insights.quote: is taken as an indicator of the dollar's global status
insights.locator: Working Paper 34888 — Drivers of Dollar Share

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34942) by Pradhan et al. finds no monotonic dollarization or de-dollarization trend in international debt-securities denomination using a comprehensive global bond dataset.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Empirically tempers both the dollarization and de-dollarization narratives for bond issuance.
insights.quote: We find no monotonic dollarization or de-dollarization

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35343) by Clayton, Maggiori & Schreger characterizes how great powers like the US and China use existing trade and financial linkages to exert geoeconomic coercion on both foreign governments and private firms.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Formal model of weaponized interdependence helps map sanction/coercion exposure across firms and sovereigns.
insights.quote: use existing trade and financial linkages of their economies to the rest of the world to exert geoeconomic power
insights.locator: Working Paper 35343 — The Targets of Geoeconomic Coercion

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35272) by Benguria, Rojas & Saffie theorizes dollar-debt dominance becomes fragile when countries hold dollar liabilities but increasingly earn yuan-linked revenues.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Currency mismatch between yuan revenues and dollar debt is a sovereign-credit fault line as trade shifts toward China.
insights.quote: countries have inherited dollar liabilities but increasingly earn yuan-linked revenues
insights.locator: Working Paper 35272 — Geopolitical Fragmentation, Sovereign Debt, and Dollar Dominance

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35225) by Horn, Reinhart & Trebesch studies two centuries of state-driven cross-border lending using a new database of 1.2 million official loans and grants by 134 governments and 70 multilateral institutions since 1790.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Historical scale of state lending informs how official creditors (esp. China) reshape EM debt restructuring today.
insights.quote: a new database covering 1.2 million official loans and grants by 134 governments and 70 multilateral institutions since 1790

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35076) by Auray et al. shows that in 2025 the correlation between one-year inflation swaps and one-year-ahead one-year forward rates turned significantly negative for the first time on record amid tariff shocks.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: An unprecedented decoupling in inflation-expectation pricing flags regime change in how markets read tariff-driven inflation.
insights.quote: the correlation between one-year inflation swaps and one-year-ahead one-year forward rates turned significantly negative for the first time on record
insights.locator: Working Paper 35076 — Tariffs and the Term Structure of Inflation Expectations

insights.claim: An NBER article by Acharya & Laarits documents that after April 2025 US tariffs (highest in a century), the S&P 500 fell more than 11 percent while the 30-year Treasury yield rose, an atypical stock-down/long-yield-up combination.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Simultaneous equity selloff and rising long yields signals Treasuries failing as a hedge — a positioning red flag.
insights.quote: the S&P 500 fell more than 11 percent. The yield on 30-year Treasury bonds rose
insights.locator: US Treasury Bonds and Trade Policy Uncertainty

insights.claim: An NBER article by Gopinath & Neiman notes the September 2025 US trade-weighted average statutory tariff rate stood at 27 percent, the highest in over a century, applied to 176 countries covering more than 70 percent of US imports.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the 2025 tariff regime — a baseline for inflation pass-through and trade-flow modeling.
insights.quote: the US trade-weighted average statutory tariff rate stood at 27 percent, the highest in over a century

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (34852) by den Besten & Känzig constructs a narrative series of plausibly exogenous US tariff changes from 1840-2024 to identify the macroeconomic effects of tariff policy.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: A long-run causal identification of tariff macro effects underpins forecasts of the current tariff cycle.
insights.locator: Working Paper 34852 — The Macroeconomic Effects of Tariffs

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35054) by Rogoff & Yang compares the 1990s Japan and contemporary China real-estate crises, noting China's real-estate contribution to growth has declined sharply since 2018, turning the sector from growth engine to major drag.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Japan-analogy framing of China's property bust shapes expectations for a prolonged balance-sheet drag.
insights.quote: turning the sector from a key engine of expansion into a major drag on economic activity
insights.locator: Working Paper 35054 — A Tale of Two Countries

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35056) by Ma & Wei finds China's economy-wide current-account surplus is driven mainly by structural factors such as an unbalanced sex ratio and uneven access, with industrial/trade policy playing a relatively limited role in the aggregate surplus.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Reframes China's surplus as structural-savings driven, implying tariffs alone won't fix the imbalance.
insights.quote: Structural factors such as an unbalanced sex ratio and uneven access
insights.locator: Working Paper 35056 — The Chinese Current Account Imbalances

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35106) by Bai et al. reconstructs multi-layered ownership chains for 161,773 firms across 159 countries (2012-2021) to trace Chinese overseas corporate ownership through offshore tax havens.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Maps the true scale and routing of Chinese global ownership — relevant to investment-screening and sanctions exposure.
insights.quote: a comprehensive micro-level dataset of 161,773 firms across 159 countries

themes.theme: Macro-finance models and AI in regulation

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35227) by Clayton & Coppola builds a graph-based deep-learning model on security-level holdings data to test whether AI can improve macroprudential financial regulation.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Early academic work on AI-driven systemic-risk supervision signals where regulators may head.
insights.quote: We study whether AI methods applied to large-scale portfolio holdings data can improve macroprudential financial regulation
insights.locator: Working Paper 35227 — The Optimal Use of AI in Financial Regulation

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35050) by Biljanovska et al. shows recessions following asset-price booms accompanied by high credit growth are deeper and longer-lasting than those following booms without strong debt accumulation.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: Credit-fueled bubbles imply worse downturns — a framework for sizing recession risk after leveraged asset booms.
insights.quote: Recessions that follow asset price booms accompanied by high credit growth are deeper and longer-lasting
insights.locator: Working Paper 35050 — A Model of Leveraged Bubbles

insights.claim: An NBER working paper (35285) by Gordon & Ryu documents that US manufacturing productivity growth collapsed from +3.3 percent annually during 1987-2010 to -0.3 percent from 2010 to 2023.
insights.speaker: NBER
insights.so_what: A stark productivity-stall figure central to debates on reshoring, import competition, and long-run growth.
insights.quote: evaporating from an annual rate of +3.3 percent during 1987-2010 to -0.3 percent from 2010 to 2023
insights.locator: Working Paper 35285 — Does the Import Invasion Explain
